keepRight.comment_placeholder: Enter a comment to share with other users.

20.description: There is more than one node in this spot. Node IDs: {var1}.
20.title: Multiple nodes on the same spot

30.description: {var1} is tagged with "{var2}" and should be a closed loop.
30.title: Non-closed area

40.description: The first node {var1} of {var2} is not connected to any other way.
40.title: Impossible oneway

41.description: The last node {var1} of {var2} is not connected to any other way.

42.description: You cannot reach {var1} because all ways leading from it are oneway.

43.description: You cannot escape from {var1} because all ways leading to it are oneway.

50.description: {var1} is very close but not connected to way {var2}.
50.title: Almost junction

60.description: {var1} uses deprecated tag "{var2}". Please use "{var3}" instead.
60.title: Deprecated tag

72.description: {var1} is not member of any way and doesn't have any tags.

73.description: {var1} has a "{var2}" tag but no "highway" tag.

75.description: {var1} has a name "{var2}" but no other tags.

90.description: {var1} is tagged as a motorway and therefore needs a "ref", "nat_ref", or "int_ref" tag.
90.title: Motorway without ref tag

100.description: {var1} is tagged as a place of worship and therefore needs a religion tag.
100.title: Place of worship without religion

110.description: {var1} is tagged as a "{var2}" and therefore needs a name tag.
110.title: Point of interest without name

130.description: {var1} is not connected to the rest of the map.
130.title: Disconnected way

150.description: {var1} of a highway and a railway needs to be tagged as "railway=crossing" or "railway=level_crossing".
150.title: Railway crossing without tag

160.description: There are ways in different layers (e.g. tunnel or bridge) meeting at {var1}.
160.title: Railway layer conflict

190.description: {var1} intersects the {var2} {var3} but there is no junction node, bridge, or tunnel.
190.title: Intersection without junction

210.description: There is an unspecified issue with self intersecting ways.
210.title: Self-intersecting way

211.description: {var1} contains more than one node multiple times. Nodes are {var2}. This may or may not be an error.

212.description: {var1} has only two different nodes and contains one of them more than once.

230.description: {var1} is a junction of ways on different layers.
230.title: Layer conflict

231.description: {var1} is a junction of ways on different layers: {var2}.

232.description: {var1} is tagged with "layer={var2}". This need not be an error but it looks strange.

270.description: {var1} is a junction of a motorway and a highway other than "motorway", "motorway_link", "trunk", "rest_area", or "construction". Connection to "service" or "unclassified" is only valid if it has "access=no/private", or it leads to a motorway service area, or if it is a "service=parking_aisle".
270.title: Unusual motorway connection

280.description: There is an unspecified issue with this boundary.
280.title: Boundary issue

282.description: The boundary of {var1} has no valid numeric admin_level. Please do not mix admin levels (e.g. "6;7"). Always tag the lowest admin_level of all boundaries.
282.title: Boundary missing admin level

283.description: The boundary of {var1} is not a closed loop.
283.title: Boundary not a closed loop

285.description: {var1} has "admin_level={var2}" but belongs to a relation with lower "admin_level" (e.g. higher priority); it should have the lowest "admin_level" of all relations.
285.title: Boundary admin_level too high

290.description: There is an unspecified issue with this restriction.
290.title: Restriction issue

292.description: {var1} has {var2} "from" members, but it should have 1.
292.title: Restriction missing "from" way

293.description: {var1} has {var2} "to" members, but it should have 1.
293.title: Restriction missing "to" way

294.description: {var1} has "from" or "to" members which should be ways. {var2}.
294.title: Restriction "from" or "to" is not a way

295.description: {var1} has a "via" (node {var2}) which is not the first or the last member of "{var3}" (way {var4}).
295.title: Restriction "via" is not an endpoint

296.description: {var1} has a restriction type "{var2}" but the angle is {var3} degrees. Maybe the restriction type is not appropriate?
296.title: Unusual restriction angle

297.description: {var1} does not match the direction of "to" way {var2}.
297.title: Wrong direction of "to" way

298.description: {var1} may be redundant. Entry already prohibited by "oneway" tag on {var2}.
298.title: Redundant restriction - oneway

300.description: {var1} is missing a "maxspeed" tag and is tagged as motorway, trunk, primary, or secondary.
300.title: Missing maxspeed

310.description: There is an unspecified issue with this roundabout.
310.title: Roundabout issue

311.description: {var1} is part of a roundabout but is not closed-loop. (Split carriageways approaching a roundabout should not be tagged as roundabout).
311.title: Roundabout not closed loop

312.description: If {var1} is in a country with {var2} traffic then its orientation goes the wrong way around.
312.title: Roundabout wrong direction

313.description: {var1} has only {var2} other road(s) connected. Roundabouts typically have 3 or more.
313.title: Roundabout weakly connected

320.description: {var1} is tagged as "{var2}" but doesn't have a connection to any other "{var3}" or "{var4}".
320.title: Improper link connection

350.description: {var1} doesn't have a tag in common with its surrounding ways that shows the purpose of this bridge. There should be one of these tags: {var2}.
350.title: Improper bridge tag

360.description: It would be nice if {var1} had a local name tag "name:XX={var2}" where XX shows the language of its common name "{var2}".
360.title: Missing local name tag

370.description: {var1} has tags in common with the surrounding way {var2} {var3} and seems to be redundant.
370.title: Doubled places

380.description: {var1} is tagged "{var2}" but has no physical tag (e.g. "leisure", "building", "amenity", or "highway").
380.title: Non-physical use of sport tag

400.description: There is an unspecified issue with the geometry here.
400.title: Geometry issue

401.description: Ways {var1} and {var2} join in a very sharp angle here and there is no oneway tag or turn restriction that prevents turning.
401.title: Missing turn restriction

402.description: {var1} bends in a very sharp angle here.
402.title: Impossible angle

410.description: There is an unspecified issue with a contact website or URL.
410.title: Website issue

411.description: {var1} may have an outdated URL: {var2} returned HTTP status code {var3}.

412.description: {var1} may have an outdated URL: {var2} contained suspicious text "{var3}".

413.description: {var1} may have an outdated URL: {var2} did not contain keywords "{var3}".

keepRight.title: KeepRight

partial.tooltip: Areas are drawn with fill only around their inner edges. (Recommended for beginner mappers)

wireframe.tooltip: Enabling wireframe mode makes it easy to see the background imagery.

background.best_imagery: Best known imagery source for this location

minimap.tooltip: Show a zoomed out map to help locate the area currently displayed.

background.offset: Drag anywhere in the gray area below to adjust the imagery offset, or enter the offset values in meters.
background.title: Background

commit.comment_needed_message: Please add a changeset comment first.
commit.outstanding_errors_message: Please resolve all errors first. {count} remaining.
commit.request_review: I would like someone to review my edits.
commit.request_review_info: Unsure about something? Invite an experienced mapper to check your work once it's live.
commit.title: Upload to OpenStreetMap
commit.upload_explanation: The changes you upload will be visible on all maps that use OpenStreetMap data.
commit.upload_explanation_with_user: The changes you upload as {user} will be visible on all maps that use OpenStreetMap data.

aerialways.tooltip: Chair Lifts, Gondolas, Flying Foxes, etc.

paths.tooltip: Foot Paths, Sidewalks, Cycle Paths, etc.

areas.add_area_command: To add an area, press the {area_icon} **{area}** button on the toolbar above the map, or press the shortcut key `3`.
areas.add_area_continue: Continue placing nodes along the edge of the area by {leftclick} clicking, {tap_icon} tapping, or pressing the `{space}` key. While drawing, you can zoom in or drag the map in order to add more detail.
areas.add_area_draw: Next, place the first corner of the area. With a mouse, position the cursor over any corner and {leftclick} left-click to place a node. On a touchscreen, {tap_icon} tap the location. Alternatively, you can also press the `{space}` key while pointing the mouse cursor at the desired position.
areas.add_area_finish: To finish an area, click or tap on the first or last node again or press `{return}`.
areas.delete_area: If an area is entirely incorrect, for example a building that doesn't exist in the real world, it's OK to delete it. Be cautious when deleting features - the background imagery you are using might be outdated, and a building that looks wrong could simply be newly built.
areas.delete_area_command: To delete an area, {rightclick} right-click or {longpress_icon} long-press the area to show the edit menu, then use the {delete_icon} **{delete}** operation.
areas.intro: *Areas* are used to show the boundaries of features like lakes, buildings, and residential areas. Areas should be traced around the edge of the feature that they represent, for example, around the base of a building.
areas.modify_area_addnode: You can also create new nodes along an area by {leftclick}**x2** double-clicking or {doubletap_icon} double-tapping an edge, or by dragging the small triangles at the midpoints between nodes.
areas.modify_area_dragnode: Often you'll see areas that aren't shaped correctly, for example a building that does not match up with the background imagery. To adjust the shape of an area, first select it. All nodes of the area will be drawn as small circles. You can then drag the nodes to better locations.
areas.point_or_area: Many features can be represented as points or areas. You should map buildings and property outlines as areas whenever possible. Place points inside a building area to represent businesses, amenities, and other features located inside the building.
areas.square_area_command: Many area features like buildings have square corners. To square the corners of an area, {rightclick} right-click or {longpress_icon} long-press the edge of the area and choose the {orthogonalize_icon} **{orthogonalize}** operation from the edit menu.
areas.title: Areas

editing.backups: If you happen to close the browser before saving, your edits are still stored locally. iD will offer to restore your work when you come back later on the same browser and device.
editing.keyboard: You can view a list of keyboard shortcuts by pressing the `{shortcuts_key}` key.
editing.multiselect: Selecting multiple features together lets you quickly edit them all at once.
editing.multiselect_lasso: Another way to select multiple features is to hold down the `{shift}` key, then press and hold down the {leftclick} left mouse button and drag the mouse to draw a selection lasso. All of the points inside the lasso area will be selected.
editing.multiselect_shift_click: With a mouse and keyboard, hold `{shift}` and {leftclick} left-click features to add them to the selection. On a touchscreen, {longpress_icon} tap-and-hold one feature while {tap_icon} tapping additional features with another finger.
editing.save: Press the {save_icon} **{save}** button to finish your edits and send them to OpenStreetMap. You should remember to save your work frequently!
editing.save_validation: On the save screen, you'll have a chance to review what you've done. iD will also perform some basic checks for missing data and may offer helpful suggestions and warnings if something doesn't seem right.
editing.select_left_click: {leftclick} Left-click or {tap_icon} tap a feature to select it. This will highlight it with a pulsing glow and display further details about it in the sidebar.
editing.select_right_click: {rightclick} Right-click or {longpress_icon} long-press a feature to display the edit menu, which provides operations such as copy, rotate, and delete.
editing.select_space: The `{space}` key can also be used instead of left-click when selecting and drawing.
editing.title: Editing & Saving
editing.undo_redo: Your edits are stored locally in your browser until you choose to save them to the OpenStreetMap server. You can undo edits by pressing the {undo_icon} **{undo}** button, and redo them by pressing the {redo_icon} **{redo}** button.
editing.upload: Before uploading your changes you must enter a [changeset comment](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Good_changeset_comments). Then press **{upload}** to send your changes to OpenStreetMap, where they will be merged into the map and publicly visible to everyone.

feature_editor.definitions: The top section displays the feature's type. The middle section contains *fields* showing the feature's attributes, such as its name or address.
feature_editor.fields_add_field: You can also use the "Add field" dropdown to add more fields, such as a description, Wikipedia link, wheelchair access, and more.
feature_editor.fields_all_fields: The "{fields}" section contains all of the feature's details that you may edit. In OpenStreetMap, all of the fields are optional, and it's OK to leave a field blank if you are unsure.
feature_editor.fields_example: Each feature type will display different fields. For example, a road may display fields for its surface and speed limit, but a restaurant may display fields for the type of food it serves and the hours it is open.
feature_editor.intro: The *feature editor* appears alongside the map, and allows you to see and edit all of the information for the selected feature.
feature_editor.tags_all_tags: Below the fields section, you can expand the "{tags}" section to edit any of the OpenStreetMap *tags* for the selected feature. Each tag consists of a *key* and *value*, data elements that define all of the features stored in OpenStreetMap.
feature_editor.tags_resources: Editing a feature's tags requires intermediate knowledge about OpenStreetMap. You should consult resources like the [OpenStreetMap Wiki](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Main_Page) or [Taginfo](https://taginfo.openstreetmap.org/) to learn more about accepted OpenStreetMap tagging practices.
feature_editor.title: Feature Editor
feature_editor.type: You can press the feature type to change the feature to a different type. Everything that exists in the real world can be added to OpenStreetMap, so there are thousands of feature types to choose from.
feature_editor.type_picker: The type picker displays the most common feature types, such as parks, hospitals, restaurants, roads, and buildings. You can search for anything by typing what you're looking for in the search box. You can also press the {inspect} **Info** icon next to the feature type to learn more about it.

about.about: This field allows you to inspect and modify turn restrictions. It displays a model of the selected intersection including other nearby connected roads.
about.from_via_to: A turn restriction always contains: one **FROM way**, one **TO way**, and either one **VIA node** or one or more **VIA ways**.
about.maxdist: The "{distField}" slider controls how far to search for additional connected roads.
about.maxvia: The "{viaField}" slider adjusts how many via ways may be included in the search. (Tip: simple is better)
about.title: About

inspecting.about: Hover over any **FROM** segment to see whether it has any turn restrictions. Each possible **TO** destination will be drawn with a coloured shadow showing whether a restriction exists.
inspecting.only: "Only" means that a vehicle taking that path may only make that choice, for example "Only Straight On".
inspecting.restricted: "Restricted" means that there is a turn restriction, for example "No Left Turn".
inspecting.title: Inspecting

modifying.about: To modify turn restrictions, first click on any starting **FROM** segment to select it. The selected segment will pulse, and all possible **TO** destinations will appear as turn symbols.
modifying.indicators: Then, click on a turn symbol to toggle it between "Allowed", "Restricted", and "Only".
modifying.title: Modifying

tips.indirect: **Some restrictions display the text "(indirect)" and are drawn lighter.**
tips.indirect_example: These restrictions exist because of another nearby restriction. For example, an "Only Straight On" restriction will indirectly create "No Turn" restrictions for all other paths through the intersection.
tips.indirect_noedit: You may not edit indirect restrictions. Instead, edit the nearby direct restriction.
tips.simple_example: For example, avoid creating a via-way restriction if a simpler via-node turn restriction will do.
tips.title: Tips

gps.intro: Collected GPS traces are a valuable source of data for OpenStreetMap. This editor supports *.gpx*, *.geojson*, and *.kml* files on your local device. You can collect GPS traces with a smartphone, sports watch, or other GPS device.
gps.survey: For information on how to perform a GPS survey, read [Mapping with a smartphone, GPS, or paper](http://learnosm.org/en/mobile-mapping/).
gps.title: GPS Traces
gps.tracing: The GPS track isn't sent to OpenStreetMap - the best way to use it is to draw on the map, using it as a guide for the new features that you add.
gps.upload: You can also [upload your GPS data to OpenStreetMap](https://www.openstreetmap.org/trace/create) for other users to use.
gps.using: To use a GPS trace for mapping, drag and drop the data file onto the map editor. If it's recognised, it will be drawn on the map as a bright purple line. Open the {data_icon} **{map_data}** panel on the side of the map to enable, disable, or zoom to your GPS data.

help.before_start: You should be familiar with OpenStreetMap and this editor before you start editing. iD contains a walkthrough to teach you the basics of editing OpenStreetMap. Press the "{start_the_walkthrough}" button on this screen to start the tutorial—it takes only about 15 minutes.
help.open_data: Edits that you make on this map will be visible to everyone who uses OpenStreetMap. Your edits can be based on personal knowledge, on-the-ground surveying, or imagery collected from aerial or street level photos. Copying from commercial sources, like Google Maps, [is strictly forbidden](https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright).
help.open_source: The iD editor is a collaborative open source project, and you are using version {version} now. The source code is available [on GitHub](https://github.com/openstreetmap/iD).
help.open_source_help: You can help iD by [translating](https://github.com/openstreetmap/iD/blob/develop/CONTRIBUTING.md#translating) or [reporting bugs](https://github.com/openstreetmap/iD/issues).
help.title: Help
help.welcome: Welcome to the iD editor for [OpenStreetMap](https://www.openstreetmap.org/). With this editor you can update OpenStreetMap right from your web browser.

imagery.choosing: To see which imagery sources are available for editing, open the {layers_icon} **{background_settings}** panel on the side of the map.
imagery.intro: The background imagery that appears beneath the map data is an important resource for mapping. This imagery can be aerial photos collected from satellites, aeroplanes, and drones, or it can be scanned historical maps or other freely available source data.
imagery.offset: Imagery is sometimes offset slightly from accurate map data. If you see a lot of roads or buildings shifted from the background imagery, it may be the imagery that's incorrect, so don't move them all to match the background. Instead, you can adjust the background so that it matches the existing data by expanding the "{imagery_offset}" section at the bottom of the Background Settings pane.
imagery.offset_change: Press the small triangle buttons to adjust the imagery offset in small steps, or hold the {leftclick} left mouse button and drag within the grey square to slide the imagery into alignment.
imagery.sources: The [Bing Maps](https://www.bing.com/maps/) satellite layer or the best local imagery is set as the default background. Depending on where you are editing, multiple imagery sources are available. Some may be newer or have higher resolution, so it is always useful to check and see which layer is the best one to use as a mapping reference.
imagery.title: Background Imagery

lines.add_line: To add a line, press the {line_icon} **{line}** button on the toolbar above the map, or press the shortcut key `2`.
lines.add_line_continue: Continue placing nodes along the shape of the line by {leftclick} clicking, {tap_icon} tapping, or pressing the `{space}` key. While drawing, you can zoom in or drag the map in order to add more detail.
lines.add_line_draw: Next, mark the line's starting location. With a mouse, position the cursor where the line should begin and {leftclick} left-click to place a node. On a touchscreen, {tap_icon} tap the location. Alternatively, you can also press the `{space}` key while pointing the mouse cursor at the desired position.
lines.add_line_finish: To finish a line, click or tap on the last node again or press `{return}`.
lines.connect_line: Having roads connected properly is important for the map and essential for providing driving directions.
lines.connect_line_display: The connections between roads are drawn with gray circles. The endpoints of a line are drawn with larger white circles if they don't connect to anything.
lines.connect_line_drag: To connect a line to another feature, drag one of the line's nodes onto the other feature until both features snap together. Tip: You can hold down the `{alt}` key to prevent nodes from connecting to other features.
lines.connect_line_tag: If you know that the connection has traffic signals or pedestrian crossings, you can add them by selecting the connecting node and using the feature editor to select the correct feature's type.
lines.delete_line: If a line is entirely incorrect, for example a road that doesn't exist in the real world, it's OK to delete it. Be careful when deleting features: the background imagery you are using might be outdated, and a road that looks wrong could simply be newly built.
lines.delete_line_command: To delete a line, {rightclick} right-click or {longpress_icon} long-press the line to show the edit menu, then use the {delete_icon} **{delete}** operation.
lines.disconnect_line_command: To disconnect a road from another feature, {rightclick} right-click or {longpress_icon} long-press the connecting node and choose the {disconnect_icon} **{disconnect}** operation from the edit menu.
lines.intro: *Lines* are used to represent features such as roads, railways, and rivers. Lines should be drawn down the centre of the feature that they represent.
lines.modify_line_addnode: You can also create new nodes along a line by {leftclick}**x2** double-clicking or {doubletap_icon} double-tapping the line, or by dragging the small triangles at the midpoints between nodes.
lines.modify_line_dragnode: Often you'll see lines that aren't shaped correctly, for example a road that does not match up with the background imagery. To adjust the shape of a line, first select it. All nodes of the line will be drawn as small circles. You can then drag the nodes to better locations.
lines.move_line_command: To move an entire line, {rightclick} right-click the line and choose the {move_icon} **{move}** operation from the edit menu. Then move the mouse, and {leftclick} left-click to place the line in a new location.
lines.move_line_connected: Lines that are connected to other features will stay connected as you move the line to a new location. iD may prevent you from moving a line across another connected line.
lines.title: Lines

notes.add_note: To add a new note, press the {note_icon} **{note}** button on the toolbar above the map, or press the shortcut key `{add_note_key}`.
notes.intro: *Notes* are used to alert other users that a feature requires fixing or attention. Notes mark a specific location on the map. To view existing notes or add new ones, open the {data_icon} **{map_data}** panel and enable the {osm_notes} layer.
notes.move_note: To move a note, {leftclick} left-click and drag it with a mouse or {touchdrag_icon} tap-and-drag it on a touchscreen. Only new notes can be moved.
notes.place_note: Next, place the new note on the map. With a mouse, position the cursor where the note should go and {leftclick} left-click. On a touchscreen, {tap_icon} tap the location.
notes.save_note: You must save any note edits individually by pressing the buttons below the note comments. Note edits are **not** included in changesets that you upload to OpenStreetMap.
notes.title: Notes
notes.update_note: An existing note can be updated by closing it, reopening it, or adding a comment to it. Closing a note indicates that the problem has been resolved. Reopening a note indicates that the original issue is not resolved.

operations.circularize: {circularize_icon} **{circularize}** turns areas and closed lines into circles.
operations.continue: {continue_icon} **{continue}** lets you extend existing lines from their endpoints.
operations.delete: {delete_icon} **{delete}** removes features from the map. Anything that doesn't exist in the real world should be deleted.
operations.disconnect: {disconnect_icon} **{disconnect}** separates connected lines and areas from each other. You can disconnect entire features or just selected points.
operations.downgrade: {downgrade_icon} **{downgrade}** removes most tags except building and address info. This is helpful when businesses close yet their facilities remain.
operations.extract: {extract_icon} **{extract}** detaches points from lines. It also pulls points out of buildings and other areas.
operations.intro: *Operations* are special commands you can use to edit features. {rightclick} Right-click or {longpress_icon} long-press any feature to view the available operations.
operations.intro_2: Each operation has its own keyboard shortcut. Most operations can be applied to multiple features at once.
operations.merge: {merge_icon} **{merge}** combines multiple features into one. You can merge areas together to create multipolygons, merge lines together to create longer lines, and merge points into anything to transfer their tags.
operations.move: {move_icon} **{move}** lets you drag features around the map.
operations.orthogonalize: {orthogonalize_icon} **{orthogonalize}** snaps the corners of areas and lines to 90°. You can square individual corners or entire features.
operations.reflect: {reflect_short_icon} **{reflect_short}** and {reflect_long_icon} **{reflect_long}** reflect features over their short and long axes.
operations.reverse: {reverse_icon} **{reverse}** changes the direction of features. The direction matters for things like one-way roads, traffic signs, waterways, and cliffs.
operations.rotate: {rotate_icon} **{rotate}** lets you swivel features around their centre points.
operations.split: {split_icon} **{split}** divides lines at the selected points. If specific lines are also selected, only those are split.
operations.straighten: {straighten_icon} **{straighten}** removes bends from lines. It can also align groups of points into neat rows.
operations.title: Operations

overview.features: We use the word *features* to describe things that appear on the map, such as roads, buildings, or points of interest. Anything in the real world can be mapped as a feature on OpenStreetMap. Map features are represented on the map using *points*, *lines*, or *areas*.
overview.navigation_zoom: You can zoom in or out by scrolling with a {mousewheel_icon} mouse wheel or trackpad, or by {pinch_icon} pinching with two fingers on a touchscreen. You can also press the {plus} / {minus} buttons along the side of the map or the `+`, `-` keys on a keyboard.
overview.nodes_ways: In OpenStreetMap, points are sometimes called *nodes*, and lines and areas are sometimes called *ways*.
overview.title: Overview

points.add_point: To add a point, press the {point_icon} **{point}** button on the toolbar above the map, or press the shortcut key `1`.
points.add_point_finish: Next, place the new point on the map. With a mouse, position the cursor where the point should go and {leftclick} left-click. On a touchscreen, {tap_icon} tap the location. Alternatively, you can also press the `{space}` key while pointing the mouse cursor at the desired position.
points.delete_point: It's OK to delete features that don't exist in the real world. Deleting a feature from OpenStreetMap removes it from the map that everyone uses, so you should make sure a feature is really gone before you delete it.
points.delete_point_command: To delete a point, {rightclick} right-click or {longpress_icon} long-press the point to show the edit menu, then use the {delete_icon} **{delete}** operation.
points.intro: *Points* can be used to represent features such as shops, restaurants, and monuments. They mark a specific location, and describe what's there.
points.move_point: To move a point, {leftclick} left-click and drag it with a mouse or {touchdrag_icon} tap-and-drag it on a touchscreen.
points.title: Points

qa.intro: *Quality Assurance* (Q/A) tools can find improper tags, disconnected roads, and other issues with OpenStreetMap, which mappers can then fix. To view existing Q/A issues, open the {data_icon} **{map_data}** panel and enable a specific Q/A layer.
qa.issues: Handling Q/A issues is similar to handling notes. Select a marker to view the issue details in the sidebar. Each tool has its own capabilities, but generally you can comment and/or close an issue.
qa.title: Quality Assurance
qa.tools: The following tools are currently supported: [KeepRight](https://www.keepright.at/) and [Osmose](https://osmose.openstreetmap.fr/).

relations.boundary: A *boundary* relation is a group of one or more line features that together form an administrative boundary.
relations.boundary_add: To add a feature to a boundary relation, select the feature and scroll down to the "{relations}" section of the feature editor, then press the {plus} add button to add this feature to a nearby existing relation or a new relation.
relations.edit_relation: At the bottom of the feature editor, you can expand the "{relations}" section to see if the selected feature is a member of any relations. You can then select a relation to edit it.
relations.edit_relation_add: To add a feature to a relation, select the feature, then press the {plus} add button in the "{relations}" section of the feature editor. You can choose from a list of nearby relations, or choose the "{new_relation}" option.
relations.edit_relation_delete: You can also press the {delete_icon} **{delete}** button to remove the selected feature from the relation. If you remove all of the members from a relation, the relation will be deleted automatically.
relations.intro: A *relation* is a special type of feature in OpenStreetMap that groups together other features. The features that belong to a relation are called *members*, and each member can have a *role* in the relation.
relations.maintain_relation: For the most part, iD will maintain relations automatically as you edit. You should take care when replacing features that might be members of relations. For example if you delete a section of road and draw a new section of road to replace it, you should add the new section to the same relations (routes, turn restrictions, etc.) as the original.
relations.multipolygon: A *multipolygon* relation is a group of one or more *outer* features and one or more inner features. The outer features define the outer edges of the multipolygon, and the inner features define sub-areas or holes cut out from the inside of the multipolygon.
relations.multipolygon_create: To create a multipolygon, for example a building with a hole in it, draw the outer edge as an area and the inner edge as a line or different kind of area. Then `{shift}`+{leftclick} left-click to select both features, {rightclick} right-click to show the edit menu, and choose the {merge_icon} **{merge}** operation.
relations.multipolygon_merge: Merging several lines or areas will create a new multipolygon relation with all selected areas as members. iD will choose the inner and outer roles automatically, based on which features are contained inside other features.
relations.route: A *route* relation is a group of one or more line features that together form a route network, like a bus route, train route, or highway route.
relations.route_add: To add a feature to a route relation, select the feature and scroll down to the "{relations}" section of the feature editor, then press the {plus} add button to add this feature to a nearby existing relation or a new relation.
relations.title: Relations
relations.turn_restriction: A *turn restriction* relation is a group of several road segments in an intersection. Turn restrictions consist of a *from* road, *via* node or roads, and a *to* road.
relations.turn_restriction_editing: In the "{turn_restrictions}" field, select a "from" road, and see whether turns are allowed or restricted to any of the "to" roads. You can press the turn icons to toggle them between allowed and restricted. iD will create relations automatically and set the from, via, and to roles based on your choices.
relations.turn_restriction_field: To edit turn restrictions, select a junction node where two or more roads meet. The feature editor will display a special "{turn_restrictions}" field containing a model of the intersection.

streetlevel.intro: Street level photos are useful for mapping traffic signs, businesses, and other details that you can't see from satellite and aerial images. The iD editor supports street level photos from a variety of different providers, such as [Panoramax](https://panoramax.fr), [Mapillary](https://www.mapillary.com), or [KartaView](https://kartaview.org).
streetlevel.photos: When enabled, the photo overlay displays a line along the sequence of photos. At higher zoom levels, a circle marks at each photo location, and at even higher zoom levels, a cone indicates the direction the camera was facing when the photo was taken.
streetlevel.title: Street Level Photos
streetlevel.using: To use street level photos for mapping, open the {data_icon} **{map_data}** panel on the side of the map to enable or disable the available photo overlays.
streetlevel.viewer: When you select one of the photo locations, a photo viewer appears in the bottom corner of the map. The photo viewer contains controls to step forward and backward in the image sequence. It also shows the username of the person who captured the image, the date it was captured, and a link to view the image on the original site.

lock.suggestion: The "{label}" field is locked because there is a Wikidata tag. You can delete it or edit the tags in the "Tags" section.

areas.add_field: This playground doesn't have an official name, so we won't add anything in the {name} field.{br}Instead let's add some additional details about the playground to the {description} field. **Open the Add Field list.**
areas.add_playground: *Areas* are used to show the boundaries of features like lakes, buildings, and residential areas.{br}They can also be used for more detailed mapping of many features you might normally map as points. **Press the {area_icon} {area} button to add a new area.**
areas.continue_playground: Continue drawing the area by placing more nodes along the playground's edge. It is OK to connect the area to the existing walking paths.{br}Tip: You can hold down the `{alt}` key to prevent nodes from connecting to other features. **Continue drawing an area for the playground.**
areas.describe_playground: **Add a description, then press the {button} button to close the feature editor.**
areas.finish_area_click: Finish the area by pressing `{return}`, or clicking again on either the first or last node.
areas.finish_area_tap: Finish the area by {tap_icon} tapping again on either the first or last node, or by pressing `{return}` on a keyboard.
areas.finish_playground: **Finish drawing an area for the playground.**
areas.play: Good job! Try drawing a few more areas, and see what other kinds of area features you can add to OpenStreetMap. **When you are ready to continue to the next chapter, press '{next}'.**
areas.retry_add_field: You didn't select the {field} field. Let's try again.
areas.start_playground: Let's add this playground to the map by drawing an area. Areas are drawn by placing *nodes* along the outer edge of the feature.
areas.starting_node_click: **Click or press `{space}` to place a starting node on one of the corners of the playground.**
areas.starting_node_tap: **{tap_icon} Tap to place a starting node on one of the corners of the playground.**
areas.title: Areas

buildings.add_building: OpenStreetMap is the world's largest database of buildings.{br}You can help improve this database by tracing buildings that aren't already mapped. **Press the {area_icon} {area} button to add a new area.**
buildings.add_tank: Next we'll trace this circular storage tank. **Press the {area_icon} {area} button to add a new area.**
buildings.building_corner_click: **Click or press `{space}` to place a starting node on one of the corners of the building.**
buildings.building_corner_tap: **{tap_icon} Tap one of the corners of the building to place a starting node.**
buildings.choose_preset_house: There are many different types of buildings, but this one is clearly a house.{br}If you're not sure of the type, it's OK to just choose the generic Building type. **Choose the {preset} type.**
buildings.circle_tank: **Press the {circularize_icon} {circularize} button to make the tank a circle.**
buildings.continue_building: Continue placing nodes to trace the outline of the building. Remember that you can zoom in if you want to add more details.
buildings.continue_tank: Add a few more nodes around the edge. The circle will be created outside the nodes that you draw.
buildings.done_square: See how the corners of the building moved into place? Let's learn another useful trick.
buildings.edit_menu_building_touch: **{longpress_icon} Long-press the building you created to show the edit menu.**
buildings.edit_menu_tank_touch: **{longpress_icon} Long-press the storage tank you created to show the edit menu.**
buildings.play: Great Job! Practice tracing a few more buildings, and try some of the other operations in the edit menu. **When you are ready to continue to the next chapter, press '{next}'.**
buildings.retry_building: It looks like you had some trouble placing the nodes at the building corners. Try again!
buildings.retry_circle: You didn't press the {circularize_icon} {circularize} button. Try again.
buildings.retry_square: You didn't press the {orthogonalize_icon} {orthogonalize} button. Try again.
buildings.rightclick_building: **{rightclick} Right-click to select the building you created and show the edit menu.**
buildings.rightclick_tank: **{rightclick} Right-click to select the storage tank you created and show the edit menu.**
buildings.square_building: The house that you just added will look even better with perfectly square corners. **Press the {orthogonalize_icon} {orthogonalize} button to tidy up the building's shape.**
buildings.start_building: Let's add this house to the map by tracing its outline.{br}Buildings should be traced around their footprint as accurately as possible.
buildings.start_tank: Don't worry, you won't need to draw a perfect circle. Just draw an area inside the tank that touches its edge.
buildings.tank_edge_click: **Click or press `{space}` to place a starting node on the edge of the tank.**
buildings.tank_edge_tap: **{tap_icon} Tap the edge of the tank to place a starting node.**
buildings.title: Buildings

lines.add_line: *Lines* are used to represent features such as roads, railways, and rivers. **Press the {line_icon} {line} button to add a new line.**
lines.add_node: We can add some nodes to this line to improve its shape. One way to add a node is to double-click the line where you want to add a node. **Double-click on the line to create a new node.**
lines.add_node_touch: We can add some nodes to this line to improve its shape. One way to add a node is to {doubletap_icon} double-tap the line where you want to add a node. **Double-tap on the line to create a new node.**
lines.add_to_selection_click: You can hold `{shift}` while clicking to select multiple things. **Shift-click on {other2}.**
lines.add_to_selection_touch: **{longpress_icon} Tap-and-hold {selected} and then {tap_icon} tap {other2} with another finger to select both.**
lines.choose_preset_residential: There are many different types of roads, but this one is a residential road. **Choose the {preset} type.**
lines.continue_drag_midpoint: This line is looking much better! Continue to adjust this line until the curve matches the road shape. **When you're happy with how the line looks, press {ok}.**
lines.continue_line: Continue drawing the line for the new road. Remember that you can drag and zoom the map if needed.
lines.delete_lines: It's OK to delete lines for roads that don't exist in the real world.{br}Here's an example where the city planned a {street} but never built it. We can improve this part of the map by deleting the extra lines.
lines.did_name_road: Looks good! Next we will learn how to update the shape of a line.
lines.did_split_multi: Good job! {street1} is now split into two pieces. The top part can be removed. **Select the top part of {street2}.**
lines.drag_to_intersection: **Drag the endpoint to the place where these roads should intersect.**
lines.finish_drag_endpoint: **Release the mouse button to finish dragging.**
lines.finish_drag_endpoint_touch: **Let go of the node to finish dragging.**
lines.finish_line_click: When you're finished, click the last node again or press `{return}`.
lines.finish_line_tap: When you're finished, {tap_icon} tap the last node again or press `{return}` on a keyboard.
lines.intersect: Continue drawing the line by placing more nodes along the road.{br}Roads, along with paths and ferry routes, must be properly connected to each other for navigation apps to work. **Place an intersection node on {name} to connect the two lines.**
lines.line_draw_info: In OpenStreetMap, lines should be drawn down the centre of the road. You can drag and zoom the map while drawing if necessary.
lines.missing_road: Here is a road that is missing. Let's add it!
lines.multi_delete: **Press the {delete_icon} {delete} button to remove the extra lines.**
lines.multi_edit_menu_touch: **{longpress_icon} Long-press on one of the lines to show the edit menu.**
lines.multi_rightclick: **{rightclick} Right-click on one of the lines to show the edit menu.**
lines.multi_select: {selected} is now selected. Let's also select {other1}.
lines.multi_select_success: Good! Both lines to delete are now selected.
lines.name_road: **Give this road a name, then press the {button} button or `{esc}` to close the feature editor.**
lines.play: Great! Use the skills that you've learned in this chapter to practice editing some more lines. **When you are ready to continue to the next chapter, press '{next}'.**
lines.retry_delete: You didn't press the {delete_icon} {delete} button. Try again.
lines.retry_intersect: The road needs to intersect {name}. Let's try again!
lines.retry_preset_residential: You didn't select the {preset} type. **Press this button to choose again.**
lines.retry_split: You didn't press the {split_icon} {split} button. Try again.
lines.split_street: The last real street is {street1}, so we will *split* {street2} at this intersection and remove everything above it.
lines.start_drag_endpoint: When a line is selected, you can adjust any of its nodes by clicking and holding down the {leftclick} left mouse button while you drag.
lines.start_drag_endpoint_touch: When a line is selected, you can {touchdrag_icon} tap-and-drag any of its nodes to adjust them.
lines.start_drag_midpoint: Small triangles are drawn at the *midpoints* between nodes. Another way to create a new node is to drag a midpoint to a new location. **Drag the midpoint triangle to create a new node along the curve of the road.**
lines.start_line: **Start a new line by clicking at the top end of this missing road.**
lines.start_line_tap: **Start a new line by {tap_icon} tapping at the top end of this missing road.**
lines.title: Lines
lines.update_line: Sometimes you will need to change the shape of an existing line. Here is a road that doesn't look quite right.

navigation.choose_street: **Choose {name} from the list to select it.**
navigation.click_townhall: All features on the map can be selected by {leftclick} clicking on them. **Click the point to select it.**
navigation.close_townhall: **Close the feature editor by pressing the {button} button in the upper corner or `{esc}`.**
navigation.editor_street: For this selected street, the feature editor shows fields like '{field1}' and '{field2}'. **Close the feature editor by pressing the {button} button or `{esc}`.**
navigation.editor_townhall: When a feature is selected, the *feature editor* is displayed alongside the map.
navigation.features: We use the word *features* to describe the things that appear on the map. Anything in the real world can be mapped as a feature on OpenStreetMap.
navigation.fields_townhall: The middle part of the feature editor contains *fields* showing the feature's attributes, such as its name and address.
navigation.map_info: The main map area shows OpenStreetMap data on top of a background.
navigation.nodes_ways: In OpenStreetMap, points are sometimes called *nodes*, and lines and areas are sometimes called *ways*.
navigation.play: Try exploring the map and selecting some other features to see what kinds of things can be added to OpenStreetMap. **When you are ready to continue to the next chapter, press '{next}'.**
navigation.points_lines_areas: Map features are represented using *points, lines, or areas.*
navigation.preset_townhall: The top part of the feature editor shows the feature's type. This point is a {preset}.
navigation.search_street: You can also search for features in the current view, or worldwide. **Search for '{name}'.**
navigation.selected_townhall: Great! The point is now selected. Selected features are drawn with a pulsing glow.
navigation.street_different_fields: The fields shown for a street are different than the fields that were shown for the town hall.
navigation.tap_townhall: All features on the map can be selected by {tap_icon} tapping on them. **Tap the point to select it.**
navigation.title: Navigation
navigation.zoom: You can zoom the map by scrolling with a {mousewheel_icon} mouse wheel or trackpad, or by pressing the {plus} / {minus} buttons. **Zoom the map!**

points.add_close: The feature editor will remember all of your changes automatically. **When you are finished adding the name, press the {button} button or `{esc}` to close the feature editor.**
points.add_name: Let's pretend that you have local knowledge of this cafe, and you know its name. **Add a name for the cafe.**
points.add_point: They mark a specific location, and describe what's there. **Press the {point_icon} {point} button to add a new point.**
points.delete: It's OK to delete features that don't exist in the real world.{br}Deleting a feature from OpenStreetMap removes it from the map that everyone uses, so you should make sure a feature is really gone before you delete it. **Press the {delete_icon} {delete} button to remove the point.**
points.edit_menu_touch: You can {longpress_icon} long-press on any feature to see the *edit menu*, which shows a list of editing operations that can be performed. **Press-and-hold the point you created to show the edit menu.**
points.feature_editor: The point is now marked as a cafe. Using the feature editor, we can add more information about the cafe.
points.fields_info: In OpenStreetMap, all of the fields are optional, and it's OK to leave a field blank if you are unsure.
points.place_point: To place the new point on the map, position your mouse cursor where the point should go, then {leftclick} left-click or press `{space}`. **Move the mouse pointer over this building, then left-click or press `{space}`.**
points.place_point_touch: To place the new point on the map, {tap_icon} tap the location where it should go. **Tap the center of this building.**
points.play: Now that you know how to create and edit points, try creating a few more points for practice! **When you are ready to continue to the next chapter, press '{next}'.**
points.points_info: *Points* can be used to represent features such as shops, restaurants, and monuments.
points.reselect: Often points will already exist, but have mistakes or be incomplete. We can edit existing points. **Select the cafe you just created.**
points.rightclick: You can {rightclick} right-click on any feature to see the *edit menu*, which shows a list of editing operations that can be performed.{br}A right-click might be the same as a control-click or two-finger click. **Right-click to select the point you created and show the edit menu.**
points.search_cafe: There are many different features that can be represented by points. The point you just added is a cafe. **Search for '{preset}'.**
points.title: Points
points.undo: You can always undo any changes up until you save your edits to OpenStreetMap. **Press the {undo_icon} {undo} button to get the point back.**
points.update: Let's fill in some more details for this cafe. You can change its name, add a cuisine, or add an address. **Change the cafe details.**
points.update_close: **When you are finished updating the cafe, press the {button} button or `{esc}` to close the feature editor.**

startediting.help: You're now ready to edit OpenStreetMap!{br}You can replay this walkthrough anytime or view more documentation by pressing the {help_icon} {help} button or the `{help_key}` key.
startediting.save: Don't forget to regularly save your changes!
startediting.shortcuts: You can view a list of commands along with their keyboard shortcuts by pressing the `{shortcuts_key}` key.
startediting.title: Start Editing

welcome.chapters: You can use the buttons below to skip chapters at any time or to restart a chapter if you get stuck. Let's begin! **Press '{next}' to continue.**
welcome.practice: All of the data in this walkthrough is just for practising, and any edits that you make in the walkthrough will not be saved.
welcome.title: Welcome
welcome.welcome: Welcome! This walkthrough will teach you the basics of editing on OpenStreetMap.
welcome.words: This walkthrough will introduce some new words and concepts. When we introduce a new word, we'll use *italics*.

highway-highway.reference: Intersecting highways should share a junction vertex.

almost_junction.message: {feature} is very close but not connected to {feature2}

self.message: {feature} ends very close to itself but does not reconnect

almost_junction.tip: Find features that should possibly be connected to other nearby features
almost_junction.title: Almost Junctions

area_as_point.message: {feature} should be an area, not a point

detached.reference: Separate points should not share a location.

close_nodes.message: Two points in {way} are very close together
close_nodes.reference: Redundant points in a way should be merged or moved apart.
close_nodes.title: Very Close Points

aeroway-building.reference: Aeroways crossing buildings should use bridges or different layers.

aeroway-highway.reference: Aeroways crossing railways should be connected.

aeroway-railway.reference: Aeroways crossing highways should be connected.

aeroway-waterway.reference: Aeroways crossing waterways should use tunnels.

bridge-bridge.reference: Crossing bridges should use different layers.

bridge-bridge_connectable.reference: Crossing bridges should be connected or use different layers.

building-building.reference: Buildings should not intersect except on different layers.

building-highway.reference: Highways crossing buildings should use bridges, tunnels, or different layers.

building-railway.reference: Railways crossing buildings should use bridges, tunnels, or different layers.

building-waterway.reference: Waterways crossing buildings should use tunnels or different layers.

highway-highway.reference: Crossing highways should use bridges, tunnels, or intersections.

highway-railway.reference: Highways crossing railways should use bridges, tunnels, or level crossings.

highway-waterway.reference: Highways crossing waterways should use bridges, tunnels, or fords.

indoor-indoor.reference: Crossing indoor features should use different levels.

indoor-indoor_connectable.reference: Crossing indoor features should be connected or use different levels.

railway-railway.reference: Crossing railways should be connected or use bridges or tunnels.

railway-waterway.reference: Railways crossing waterways should use bridges or tunnels.

crossing_ways.tip: Find features that incorrectly cross over one another
crossing_ways.title: Crossing Ways

tunnel-tunnel.reference: Crossing tunnels should use different layers.

tunnel-tunnel_connectable.reference: Crossing tunnels should be connected or use different layers.

waterway-waterway.reference: Crossing waterways should be connected or use tunnels.

curb_nodes.footway-highway: Footways crossing highways should have separately mapped kerb nodes.

reference.text: Crossing ways should have individual kerb nodes (where appropriate). Use photo overlays to help determine kerb type.

curb_nodes.title: Kerb Nodes

message.one: {highway} is disconnected from other roads and paths
message.other: {count} routable features are connected only to each other

routable.reference: All roads, paths, and ferry routes should connect to form a single routing network. This feature (or small collection of features) is either disconnected from the routing network or only connected to it via a feature that few applications consider routable.

disconnected_way.tip: Find unroutable roads, paths, and ferry routes
disconnected_way.title: Disconnected Ways

dupe_node_on_road.tip: Nodes are less than 2 metres away.
dupe_node_on_road.tip_merge: Nodes are less than 2 metres away; you may want to merge them.
dupe_node_on_road.tip_move_away: Nodes are less than 2 metres away but not mergeable; you may want to move them further apart.

connect_endpoints.annotation: Connected the endpoints of a way.
connect_endpoints.title: Connect the ends

convert_to_line.annotation: Converted an area to a line.
convert_to_line.title: Convert this to a line

merge_close_vertices.annotation: Merged very close points in a way.

tag_as_disconnected.annotation: Tagged very close features as disconnected.
tag_as_disconnected.title: Tag as disconnected

tag_as_unsquare.annotation: Tagged a way as having unsquare corners.
tag_as_unsquare.title: Tag as physically unsquare

fixme_tag.reference: A "fixme" tag indicates that a mapper has requested help with a feature.

generic_name.reference: Names should be the actual, on-the-ground names of features.

help_request.tip: Find features where others requested assistance
help_request.title: Help Requests

end.reference: One-way roads must lead to other roads.

start.reference: One-way roads must be accessible via other roads.

impossible_oneway.tip: Find route issues with one-way features
impossible_oneway.title: Impossible One-Ways

connected.reference: Waterway segments should all flow in the same direction.

start.message: {feature} flows away from a connected waterway

reference.amap: Amap products are proprietary and must not be used as references.
reference.baidu: Baidu products are proprietary and must not be used as references.
reference.google: Google products are proprietary and must not be used as references.

incompatible_source.tip: Find features with suspicious source tags
incompatible_source.title: Suspicious Sources

line_as_area.message: {feature} should be a line, not an area

line_as_point.message: {feature} should be a line, not a point

mismatched_geometry.reference: Most features are limited to certain geometry types.
mismatched_geometry.tip: Find features with conflicting tags and geometry
mismatched_geometry.title: Mismatched Geometry

multipolygon.reference: Multipolygon members must have an inner or outer role.

missing_role.tip: Find relations with missing or incorrect member roles
missing_role.title: Missing Roles

missing_tag.reference: Features must have tags that define what they are.

relation_type.message: {feature} is a relation without a type

missing_tag.tip: Find features that are missing descriptive tags
missing_tag.title: Missing Tags

message.edits_in_view: Your edits in view look fine

old_multipolygon.reference: Multipolygons should be tagged on their relation, not their outer way.

incomplete.reference: Some features should have additional tags.

noncanonical_brand.message: {feature} looks like a common feature with nonstandard tags
noncanonical_brand.message_incomplete: {feature} looks like a common feature with incomplete tags
noncanonical_brand.reference: Some features, for example retail chains or post offices, are expected to have certain tags in common.

outdated_tags.reference: Some tags change over time and should be updated.
outdated_tags.tip: Find features with deprecated tags that can be updated
outdated_tags.title: Outdated Tags

point_as_area.message: {feature} should be a point, not an area

point_as_line.message: {feature} should be a point, not a line

point_as_vertex.message: {feature} should be a standalone point based on its tags
point_as_vertex.reference: Some features shouldn't be part of lines or areas.

contact.message: {feature} might be tagged with private contact information

private_data.reference: Sensitive data like personal phone numbers should not be tagged.
private_data.tip: Find features that may contain private information
private_data.title: Private Information

suspicious_name.tip: Find features with generic or suspicious names
suspicious_name.title: Suspicious Names

tag_suggests_area.message: {feature} should be a closed area based on the tag "{tag}"

unclosed_multipolygon_part.reference: All inner and outer parts of multipolygons should have connected endpoints.

unknown_road.reference: Roads without a specific type may not appear in maps or routing.

buildings.reference: Buildings with unsquare corners can often be drawn more accurately.

unsquare_way.tip: Find features with unsquare corners that can be drawn better
unsquare_way.title: Unsquare Corners (up to {val}°)

vertex_as_point.message: {feature} should be attached to a line or area based on its tags
vertex_as_point.reference: Some features shouldn't be standalone points.

map_data.autohidden: These features have been automatically hidden because too many would be shown on the screen.  You can zoom in to edit them.

history_panel.title: Show History Panel
history_panel.tooltip: Show version details for the selection.

custom.title: Custom Map Data
custom.tooltip: Drag and drop a data file onto the page, or click the button to setup

measurement_panel.title: Show Measurement Panel
measurement_panel.tooltip: Show geometry values for the selection.

map_data.osmhidden: These features have been automatically hidden because the OpenStreetMap layer is hidden.
map_data.title: Map Data

conflict.deleted: This feature has been deleted by {user}.
conflict.location: This feature was moved by both you and {user}.
conflict.memberlist: Relation members were changed by both you and {user}.
conflict.nodelist: Nodes were changed by both you and {user}.
conflict.tags: You changed the "{tag}" tag to "{local}" and {user} changed it to "{remote}".

add_area.description: Add parks, buildings, lakes or other areas to the map.
add_area.title: Area

add_line.description: Add roads, streets, footpaths, canals or other lines to the map.
add_line.title: Line

add_note.description: Spotted an issue? Let other mappers know.
add_note.title: Note

add_point.description: Add restaurants, monuments, post boxes or other points to the map.
add_point.title: Point

drag_node.connected_to_hidden: This can't be edited because it is connected to a hidden feature.

note.inputPlaceholder: Enter a comment to share with other users.
note.login: You must log in to change or comment on this note.
note.title: Edit note
note.upload_explanation: Your comments will be publicly visible to all OpenStreetMap users.
note.upload_explanation_with_user: Your comments as {user} will be publicly visible to all OpenStreetMap users.

annotation.vertex: Added a node to a way.

annotation.one: Added a member to a relation.
Changed the roles of {n} relation members.
annotation.other: Changed the roles of {n} relation members.

from_point.to_area: Moved a point to an area.
from_point.to_line: Moved a point to a line.
from_point.to_vertex: Merged a point with a point in a way.

from_vertex.to_adjacent_vertex: Merged adjacent points in a way.
from_vertex.to_area: Connected a way to an area.
from_vertex.to_line: Connected a way to a line.
from_vertex.to_point: Connected a way to a point.

connect.relation: These features can't be connected because they have conflicting relation roles.
connect.restriction: These features can't be connected because it would damage a "{relation}" relation.

continue.multiple: Several lines can be continued here. Add one to the selection to continue.
continue.not_eligible: No line can be continued here.
continue.title: Continue

too_large.one: This can't be copied because not enough of it is currently visible.
too_large.other: These can't be copied because not enough of them are currently visible.

annotation.vertex: Deleted a node from a way.

annotation.one: Removed a member from a relation.

disconnect.connected_to_hidden: This can't be disconnected because it is connected to a hidden feature.

multiple_points.multiple_ways: Disconnect the selected features at these points.
multiple_points.no_ways: Disconnect the features at these points.

single_way.area: Disconnect the selected area at these points.
single_way.line: Disconnect the selected line at these points.

multiple_ways.conjoined: Disconnect these features from each other.

single_way.area: Disconnect this area from other features.
single_way.line: Disconnect this line from other features.

single_point.multiple_ways: Disconnect the selected features at this point.
single_point.no_ways: Disconnect the features at this point.

single_way.area: Disconnect the selected area at this point.
single_way.line: Disconnect the selected line at this point.

disconnect.not_connected: There aren't enough lines/areas here to disconnect.
disconnect.not_downloaded: This can't be disconnected because parts of it have not yet been downloaded.
disconnect.relation: This can't be disconnected because it connects members of a relation.
disconnect.title: Disconnect

too_large.multiple: These can't be disconnected because not enough of them are currently visible.
too_large.single: This can't be disconnected because not enough of it is currently visible.

address.one: Downgraded a feature to an address.

building.one: Downgraded a feature to a basic building.

description.building_address: Remove all non-address and non-building tags.

merge.conflicting_relations: These features can't be merged because they belong to conflicting relations.
merge.conflicting_tags: These features can't be merged because some of their tags have conflicting values.
merge.damage_relation: These features can't be merged because it would damage a "{relation}" relation.
merge.incomplete_relation: These features can't be merged because at least one hasn't been fully downloaded.
merge.not_adjacent: These features can't be merged because their endpoints aren't connected.
merge.paths_intersect: These features can't be merged because the resulting path would intersect itself.
merge.relation: These features can't be merged because they have conflicting relation roles.
merge.title: Merge
merge.too_many_vertices: These features can't be merged because the resulting path would have too many points.

annotation.vertex: Moved a node in a way.

feature.one: Squared the corners of a feature.
Flipped a feature across its long axis.
feature.other: Flipped {n} features across their long axis.

feature.one: Flipped a feature across its short axis.
feature.other: Flipped {n} features across their short axis.

description.features: Flip the directions of these features.
description.line: Make this line go in the opposite direction.
description.lines: Make these lines go in the opposite direction.
description.point: Flip the direction of this point.
description.points: Flip the direction of these points.

area.one: Split the edge of an area.

split.connected_to_hidden: This can't be split because it is connected to a hidden feature.

multiple.multiple_node: Divide the edges of these areas at these points.
multiple.single_node: Divide the edges of these areas at this point.

single.multiple_node: Divide the edge of this area at these points.
single.single_node: Divide the edge of this area into two at this point.

multiple.multiple_node: Divide these features at these points.
multiple.single_node: Divide these features at this point.

multiple.multiple_node: Divide all lines at these points. Tip: To limit this operation to a specific line, select the line as well as the points before performing the split.
multiple.single_node: Divide all lines at this point. Tip: To limit this operation to a specific line, select both the line and point before performing the split.

single.multiple_node: Divide this line at these points.
single.single_node: Divide this line into two at this point.

split.not_eligible: Lines can't be split at their beginning or end.
split.title: Split

fromDate.title: From
fromDate.tooltip: Show photos taken after this date

toDate.title: To
toDate.tooltip: Show photos taken before this date

username_filter.title: Username
username_filter.tooltip: Show only photos by this user

third_party_icons.tooltip: Uncheck this box to avoid loading icons from third party sites such as Wikimedia Commons, Facebook, or Twitter.

rapid_menu.about_the_catalog:
These datasets have been provided as open data by various organisations and the Esri user community for the purpose of improving OpenStreetMap.
You can learn more by clicking the links below, or visiting [the Rapid Guide](https://github.com/facebookmicrosites/Open-Mapping-At-Facebook/wiki/Esri-ArcGIS-FAQ) or [Esri/ArcGIS dataset page on the OSM Wiki](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Esri/ArcGIS_Datasets).

places.description: Contains over 53 million point representations of real-world entities: schools, businesses, hospitals, religious organisations, landmarks, mountain peaks, and much more.

restore.description: Do you wish to restore unsaved changes from a previous editing session?
restore.heading: You have unsaved changes

conflict.help: Another user changed some of the same map features you changed. Click on each feature below for more details about the conflict, and choose whether to keep your changes or the other user's changes.

save.error: Errors occurred while trying to save
save.help: Review your changes and upload them to OpenStreetMap, making them visible to other users.
save.title: Save
save.unknown_error_details: Please ensure you are connected to the Internet.

instructions.info: Enter a tile URL template below.

tokens.wkid: `{wkid}`: same as proj, but without the EPSG (`3857` only)

file.instructions:
Choose a local data file. Supported types are:
   .gpx, .kml, .geojson, .json
file.label: Browse files

url.instructions:
Enter a data file URL or vector tile URL template. Valid tokens are:
   {zoom} or {z}, {x}, {y} for Z/X/Y tile scheme

circularize.label: Circularise a closed line or area

source_switch.lose_changes: You have unsaved changes. Switching the map server will discard them. Are you sure you want to switch servers?

splash.privacy: {updateMessage} By using this software, you agree to do so in accordance with the {privacyLink}.
splash.privacy_update: Our privacy policy has recently been updated.
splash.text: iD is a friendly but powerful tool for contributing to the world's best free world map. This is version {version}. For more information see {website} and report bugs at {github}.
splash.welcome: Welcome to the iD OpenStreetMap editor

success.missing: Is something missing from this list?
success.thank_you: Thank you for improving the map.
success.thank_you_location: Thank you for improving the map around {where}.

whats_new.text_v25: This release includes a new validation to make mapping kerbs easier, new datasets in the data catalogue, and upgrades to the renderer for better performance.
